title: Realm Current Tensions
description: One terse gazetteer line on what strains a realm right now — for rollable realm entries.
entries: The succession is disputed between two cousins; the levies drill in rival colors and the smiths take deposits from both.
The crown owes the mining guild three years' silver; the guild has begun, delicately, to mention it in public.
A new faith spreads in the port towns faster than the temples can denounce it; the harvest festival was half empty.
The border survey is finished and nobody likes it; both realms have appealed to an arbiter neither trusts.
Bread is dear and the granary accounts don't balance; the mob knows an official's name and chants it.
The old king lingers; the heir holds a shadow court in the summer palace and the ministries answer letters from both.
A general returned too popular from the last war; the crown weighs a triumph against a quiet posting to the far marches.
The peasants' petition against the mill-tax gathered forty thousand marks; its three authors are celebrated, and missing.
Two great houses feud over a bride, a bridge, and an insult in that ascending order; the duels have gone from first blood to funerals.
The mercenary companies wintering in the south are owed and idle — the treasury calls it a payment schedule, the towns call it occupation.
A charter city refuses the new levy, citing a privilege older than the dynasty; the crown's lawyers agree, which is the problem.
Refugees from a burned realm crowd the eastern towns; charity is strained, rents are up, and preachers work both sentiments.
The mage college defied a summons and sealed its gates; the siege is polite, expensive, and in its second year.
Counterfeit coin of excellent quality is everywhere; the mint swears innocence, and melts the evidence.
The queen's marriage treaty promised a port the admiralty will not surrender; the wedding is postponed a third time.
A prophet in the hills names the harvest moon as the end of the age; the grain markets believe enough to matter.
The heir converted to the sea-god's rite; the land temples have not said 'usurper', only spelled it.
Plague quarantine has closed the great fair for two seasons; the smuggling roads are now better maintained than the royal ones.
The frontier legions elected a spokesman, a thing without precedent; the capital debates whether to honor or hang him.
An imperial neighbor 'offers protection' with each embassy, more warmly every time; the council splits between defiance and terms.
The great river changed course and took the tariff line with it; two customs houses now tax the same barges, at war by paperwork.
A noble house was caught selling maps of the border forts; the trial must be public, the evidence cannot be.
The apprentices' compact struck the looms in three cities; the cloth halls are dark and the masters' patience theatrical.
Last year's victory delivered a province that does not want to be delivered; garrisoning it costs more than it pays.
The regent rules well for a king who is no longer young enough for a regent; ministers avoid the word by athletic circumlocution.
The banner-lords owe forty days' service and are counting them very slowly this year.
A royal favorite rises without office, title, or visible merit; the old ministers resign in ones and twos, which the favorite counts.
The census found a third fewer hearths than the tax rolls; someone has been paid for villages of ghosts, and the auditors ride out armed.
The high judge annulled a royal decree, the first in a century; the crown obeyed, wrote a careful precedent, and doubled its lawyers.
Both heirs died in the same storm at sea; the realm holds its breath through the longest genealogy in its history.
The tribute to the mountain — dragon, giant, or god, the ministries no longer agree which — was refused by vote; the realm awaits the response.
Peace, prosperity, and a bored nobility; the chroniclers agree this is how the last three wars began.
